content.body: thought when drafting the 2nd requirements, which will affect all past the first: do we want flatter or deeper structure in the requirements sections?
eg 5.3 SBD with a set of reqs under it across TLS, Extensions, Isolation, etc
TLS are uniquely coded reqs under the subclause level (5.3) so I'll do that for now
sold: current approach future-proofs against renumbering

Draft PR for extension requirements. Will be adding applicability, examples and editor notes, but putting this here now for early feedback on the top-level requirements. https://forge.etsi.org/rep/cyber/stan4cr2/en-304-617/-/merge_requests/44/diffs
littledan: Where can I find the letter you wrote to Cross-Vertical Crypto Task Force folk about Annex K?
content.formatted_body: littledan: Where can I find the letter you wrote to Cross-Vertical Crypto Task Force folk about Annex K?

content.body:
Here it is:

Hello Gisela and others,

I'm not sure what is the right way to submit a comment related to Annex K, but I wanted to give feedback with respect to web browsers. The web browser vertical rapporteur group discussed Annex K in our most recent meeting, and while we did not adopt a particular position, I would personally say, the following, which I believe matches the feeling of some other delegates:

It is important for web browser manufacturers to be able to use new crypto algorithms and protocols based on their own risk assessment, without the algorithms/protocols being present in ACM+Annex X, while still fully applying the vertical standard.

We're currently reviewing the ACM and Annex X to assess its suitability for web browsers and will share more feedback as soon as it is available. My apologies for not making it to Annex K meetings in the past.

Thanks,
Dan
we should actually submit this as a document to attach to the meeting, and we need to do it before tomorrow
yeah let's not change the heading structure; we already have the requirements numbering structure which you noticed means we don't need to renumber

I'm currently looking at what I /think/ is the latest Annex K draft V 0.0.7 (2026-04-10), and it does look like it has provision for things not listed in the ACM catalog (CRY-SOTA-unlisted), but I'm not sure K.1.2.2.4 is flexible enough. Digging in a bit now.

My off the cuff comments are: (emphasis mine)

"Identification of the certain algorithm by reference to publicly available referenced algorithm catalogues or publications issued by a recognized body and **stable for the last two years** as for example" 

"stable for 2 years" may be inconsistent with how internet standards are produced and evolve.  e.g. a quick search suggests TLS 1.3 had its last draft submission March 2018, published as RFC 8446 in August 2018, and enabled by default in Chrome in October 2018.  Maybe that's OK if we're just talking about low-level primitives vs protocols like TLS
content.formatted_body:
My off the cuff comments are: (emphasis mine)

"Identification of the certain algorithm by reference to publicly available referenced algorithm catalogues or publications issued by a recognized body and stable for the last two years as for example"

"stable for 2 years" may be inconsistent with how internet standards are produced and evolve. e.g. a quick search suggests TLS 1.3 had its last draft submission March 2018, published as RFC 8446 in August 2018, and enabled by default in Chrome in October 2018. Maybe that's OK if we're just talking about low-level primitives vs protocols like TLS

content.body:
"publicly available referenced cryptographic algorithms specified in industry standards, that are feasible and suitable for the use case;"

On the face of it, that sounds like pointing to an IETF doc should be fine, since it just says "industry standards" without defining it narrowly.

The Assessment verdict section has examples for some of the other K.1.2.2.4 options, such as "use case sector specific public available cryptographic algorithm catalogue", but not "publicly available referenced cryptographic algorithms specified in industry standards, that are feasible and suitable for the use case", which would have been nice to see to help clarify, but perhaps provides more flexibility if it's not there

With the caveat that I've not dug into Annex K before just now, it doesn't look as inflexible as I'd feared. 

Has anybody else given it a look and have the same or a different conclusion?

A quick cmd-F for "year" in the submission attached to the meeting tomorrow shows that Annex-K-07-comments-CAZ from Cadzow Communications already called out 'Remove “and stable for the last two years as” from the current text', so if that comes up it would be good to support that removal.

the thing is, the "use case sector specific public available cryptographic algorithm catalogue" is supposed to be in the vertical which we're writing right now, not just any catalogue out there on the internet

we don't yet know when the next chance we will have to revise this standard will be. It will be on a schedule driven by the European Commission. They say that they will turn around soon and ask for another version, and we should push for this to be true. But in the optimistic case we're talking about a 1-2 year cycle
would be interesting to investigate past examples for algorithms which we could reference, though that protocol example is already very interesting.
maybe we could submit a document supporting this. I'm not sure whether the other part of my fear there is overblown
Do you think Chrome will send anyone to the meeting?
Hopefully, but I've not got a firm commitment yet I'm afraid.

Ah, I read: "Identification of the certain algorithm by reference to publicly available referenced algorithm catalogues or publications issued by a recognized body and stable for the last two years as for example"

to be "publicly available referenced [in the documentation being presented as evidence]"

but it is in fact:  "publicly available referenced [in the vertical standard]

* Ah, I read: "Identification of the certain algorithm by reference to publicly available referenced algorithm catalogues or publications issued by a recognized body and stable for the last two years as for example"

to be "publicly available referenced \[in the documentation being presented as evidence\]"

but it is in fact:  "publicly available referenced \[in the vertical standard\] ?
